title: Chapter 1 (intermediate) — The architecture of a game
meta.desc: Intermediate level: the game loop and the timestep, scenes as a state machine, the simulation/rendering split, and the module layout of Tower Duel.
hero.title: The architecture of a game
hero.lead: We revisit Batch 1 with a developer's eye: the loop and the timestep, scenes as a state machine, and the big idea that shapes the whole project — separating simulation from rendering.
s1.h2: The loop, and the trap of time
s1.lead: Every real-time game is a loop: read the inputs → update the state → draw, again and again. The subtlety lives in “update”.
s1.p1: If you move an object by a fixed number of pixels each frame, its speed depends on the frame rate: twice as fast on a 120 Hz screen as on a 60 Hz one. The fix is the timestep (dt): we multiply each movement by the time that actually elapsed, in seconds.
s1.code.cap: the timestep makes the game independent of the machine
s1.callout: Two clocks. The client draws as fast as the screen allows (often 60 fps). But the match advances at a fixed pace on the server — more on that in chapter 3. Keeping these two clocks distinct is an architectural decision, not a detail.
s2.p1: Phaser organises the game into scenes. Seen from higher up, it's a finite state machine: at any moment one scene is active, and explicit transitions lead from one to another.
s2.fig.cap: The device type picks the branch: a big screen hosts (TV), a phone joins (Controller).
s2.p2: Each scene has a clear life cycle: init (receive data), create (build the display), update (every frame), and a SHUTDOWN where we clean up (remove listeners, DOM elements). Forgetting that cleanup is a guaranteed memory leak whenever you move from one scene to another.
s3.p1: The project's most important decision: the core of the game does not know about Phaser. All the logic — trajectories, collisions, wind, score — lives in src/sim/, in plain JavaScript, with no graphics dependency whatsoever.
s3.li1: 🧠 src/sim/ — the simulation: what is happening? (can run under Node, with no browser)
s3.li2: 🎨 src/scenes/, src/objects/ — the rendering: what does it look like?
s3.li3: ⚙️ src/config/ — the data: settings and themes, kept apart from the code.
s3.callout: Why bother? Because the same simulation will run on the server (the sole judge of the match) while staying testable and replayable. Decoupling the “what” from the “how” is what makes the multi-screen mode of Batch 3 possible, without rewriting the physics.
s4.h2: Docker, seen as a reproducible boundary
s4.p1: The Dockerfile is multi-stage for an architectural reason: to separate build time from run time. The first stage installs all the tooling and compiles; the second keeps only the bare minimum needed to run.
s4.p2: The result: a smaller image, with no compilers or development dependencies in production. The same logic drives the server bundle (zip / tar.gz) added in v1.1.0: ship only dist/, server/ and the shared simulation.
s4.callout: Remember. The architecture of Batch 1 fits in one sentence: a time-driven loop, scenes as a state machine, and a simulation decoupled from rendering — the foundation everything else is built on.
